original_hypothesis.title: Immutable exchangeProxy Cannot Be Updated If 0x Proxy Is Upgraded Maliciously, Risking All LRT Approvals
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The exchangeProxy (0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF) is set as immutable in the constructor and is the 0x TransparentUpgradeableProxy. During every LRT claim, _fillQuote approves this proxy for the sell token amount. The 0x proxy is upgradeable by 0x governance. If 0x governance is compromised or acts maliciously, a new implementation could steal all approved tokens. Since exchangeProxy is immutable, PrelaunchPoints cannot switch to a different swap router. Additionally, _fillQuote never resets approvals after swaps, so partial fills leave residual approvals that persist across transactions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The combination of immutable external dependency on an upgradeable proxy, persistent token approvals, and no ability to change the exchange creates a systemic risk. The approval-not-reset pattern (INV-STATE-007) compounds this by leaving dangling approvals.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol line 10: EXCHANGE_PROXY = 0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF (0x Exchange Proxy, a TransparentUpgradeableProxy on Ethereum mainnet)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _fillQuote approves exchangeProxy for _amount but never resets to 0 after swap; exchangeProxy is immutable and points to upgradeable 0x proxy

preconditions: 0x Exchange Proxy is upgraded with malicious implementation (external compromise)
Users have locked LRT tokens and are in claim phase
Any prior _fillQuote call left residual approvals (partial fills)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) sets approval but no approval reset to 0 after swap
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised 0x proxy implementation calls transferFrom to drain approved tokens from contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All LRT tokens held by contract at risk of theft

economic_questions: What is the total value of LRT tokens held by the contract during claim phase?
Does the 0x proxy typically consume full approval in standard swaps?
What are the 0x governance controls and upgrade timelock?
validation_requirements: Check if standard 0x swaps consume full approval (no residual)
Verify no approval reset after _fillQuote completion
Assess 0x proxy upgrade governance and timelock

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All LRT tokens held by the contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When maximum LRT tokens are locked and unclaimed
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of all unclaimed LRT tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LRT depositors who haven't claimed yet
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value of all locked LRT tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify residual approval state after typical 0x swap
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test partial fill scenarios and resulting approval state

validation_result.mechanism: residual approval on upgradeable external proxy
validation_result.impact_summary: _fillQuote never resets token approvals to 0 after the swap, leaving residual approvals on the immutable-but-externally-upgradeable 0x Exchange Proxy. If the proxy is upgraded maliciously or partial fills occur, approved tokens held by the contract can be drained.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies two compounding code defects:

1. **Missing approval reset in _fillQuote**: At PrelaunchPoints.sol line 374, `_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)` sets the approval before the swap. After the swap completes (line 376-378), the function never resets the approval to 0. If the 0x proxy does not consume the full allowance (partial fill scenario), the residual approval persists indefinitely. This is a well-known security anti-pattern (approve-and-forget).

2. **Immutable reference to upgradeable proxy**: `exchangeProxy` is declared `immutable` (line 31) and set in the constructor to `0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF`, which is the 0x Exchange Proxy — a TransparentUpgradeableProxy on Ethereum mainnet. The PrelaunchPoints contract has no mechanism to update this address if the 0x proxy is compromised.

The combination means: (a) residual approvals can accumulate on the exchangeProxy address, and (b) the contract can never revoke trust from this address or switch to an alternative. A malicious upgrade of the 0x proxy could call `transferFrom` on any LRT token for which the contract has residual approvals, draining those tokens from PrelaunchPoints.

Even without the 0x compromise scenario, the missing approval reset is a standalone code defect — it violates the principle of least privilege by leaving unnecessary token approvals active after operations complete.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal points to lines 371-383, which correctly identify the _fillQuote function. The code at line 374 sets approval: `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount));`. After the swap call at line 376 and the success check, no approval reset occurs. The function ends with the SwappedTokens event emission at line 382. The exchangeProxy immutability is confirmed at line 31: `address public immutable exchangeProxy;`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Approval is set to _amount at line 374 but never reset to 0 after the swap completes. If the 0x proxy performs a partial fill, residual allowance remains. Combined with the fact that exchangeProxy is immutable and points to an upgradeable 0x proxy, there is no way to revoke this trust or clear dangling approvals.
exchangeProxy is set as immutable. If the 0x proxy at this address is upgraded maliciously, PrelaunchPoints has no ability to change the swap router or revoke approvals granted to it.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls claim(LRT_token, percentage, exchange, data) after startClaimDate
8. Function returns WITHOUT resetting approval to 0
9. Any unconsumed allowance persists on exchangeProxy for this token
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from claim -> _claim -> _fillQuote shows that every LRT claim operation approves the exchangeProxy for the sell token amount. After the swap call at line 376, the function calculates boughtETHAmount and emits an event but never calls `_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, 0)`. This means if the 0x swap is a partial fill (consuming less than _amount of tokens), the remaining approval persists. The contract continues to hold these LRT tokens (for other users), and the dangling approval allows the exchangeProxy to transferFrom those tokens. Since exchangeProxy is immutable (line 31) and points to the 0x TransparentUpgradeableProxy, a governance compromise of 0x could exploit all residual approvals.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol line 10 confirms: EXCHANGE_PROXY = 0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF. This is the 0x Exchange Proxy, a known TransparentUpgradeableProxy on Ethereum mainnet.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires two conditions: (1) a partial fill leaves residual approval on the 0x proxy — this is plausible in DeFi swap scenarios, and (2) the 0x proxy is upgraded maliciously — this requires 0x governance compromise which is a high-cost external prerequisite. However, the code defect exists regardless of whether exploitation is economically feasible. The missing approval reset is a concrete code bug that violates the security best practice of minimizing token approvals. Even in normal operation, residual approvals represent unnecessary risk exposure for the contract's held LRT tokens.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The residual approvals expose LRT tokens held by the contract to the risk of unauthorized transfer via the exchangeProxy. The total value at risk equals the sum of all LRT tokens held by the contract that have had _fillQuote called on them with partial fills. In the worst case (0x proxy compromise), all LRT tokens with any residual approval could be drained. The immutability of exchangeProxy means there is no remediation path other than emergency withdrawal of all tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Add `_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, 0);` after the swap call in _fillQuote to clear any residual approval
Consider using safeIncreaseAllowance/safeDecreaseAllowance pattern instead of direct approve
Add a governance function to update exchangeProxy (with appropriate timelock/access control), or make it non-immutable with an owner update mechanism
Alternatively, track exact allowance consumed and verify it matches _amount after the swap
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy PrelaunchPoints with a mock exchangeProxy that performs partial fills (consumes only half the approved amount). 2. Lock LRT tokens for a user. 3. Complete the lifecycle (setLoopAddresses, convertAllETH). 4. Call claim with an LRT token. 5. After _fillQuote executes, check IERC20(token).allowance(prelaunchPoints, exchangeProxy) — should show residual approval equal to _amount minus consumed. 6. Demonstrate that the exchangeProxy can call transferFrom on the remaining allowance to drain tokens from the contract.
uncertainties: Whether 0x Exchange Proxy swaps typically consume 100% of the approved amount or can result in partial fills
The governance model and timelock for 0x Exchange Proxy upgrades on mainnet
Whether the specific LRT tokens used support the standard approve pattern without issues
additional_testing_needed: Test with 0x API to determine if partial fills are possible with the UniswapV3 and TransformERC20 selectors
Verify actual residual allowance after typical 0x swaps on forked mainnet
Check if any LRT tokens have non-standard approve behavior that would interact with this pattern
